text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies a healthy colonic loop alongside a large parastomal hernia. Minimal adhesions from previous surgery are visible.

The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to divide pelvic adhesions between small bowel loops and the rectal stump. Minor adhesion bands in the left lower quadrant are also divided, and the end of the colostomy is carefully freed from the anterolateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon up to the splenic flexure, ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis. The base of the colonic mesentery is also mobilized, with the dissection kept high to prevent injury to the ureter or colonic vessels.

The rectal stump is carefully isolated from the posterior aspect of the bladder using blunt dissection. A circular stapler is inserted through the anus, and an anastomosis is performed using the Knight-Griffin double stapling technique. The colon reaches the pelvis without tension, and care is taken to ensure it is not twisted before the stapler is closed, fired, and gently removed through the anus.
A drain is placed in the pelvis at the end of the operation. The parietal peritoneum is closed using intracorporeal sutures and ProTack fixation devices.
